AAA: Abdominal Aortic Aneurysm — a dangerous swelling of the main blood vessel in the abdomen. Rupture is life-threatening.
ABCDE: A structured approach to assessing sick patients: Airway, Breathing, Circulation, Disability (neurological), Exposure. Used in all emergencies.
ABG: Arterial Blood Gas — a blood test taken from an artery (usually the wrist) that measures oxygen, carbon dioxide, and acid levels in the blood.
abx: Short for antibiotics — medicines that fight bacterial infections.
ACEi: ACE inhibitor — a type of blood pressure medication (e.g. ramipril, enalapril).
ACR: Albumin-to-Creatinine Ratio — a urine test to detect early kidney damage. High levels suggest protein is leaking through the kidneys.
acidosis: When the blood becomes too acidic (pH below 7.35). Can be dangerous if severe.
ACS: Acute Coronary Syndrome — an umbrella term for when blood flow to the heart is suddenly blocked, including heart attacks.
acute: Sudden onset, usually short-term. The opposite of chronic.
acute abdomen: A sudden, severe abdominal pain that may need emergency surgery. Can be caused by appendicitis, bowel perforation, or ruptured aneurysm.
ADLs: Activities of Daily Living — basic tasks like washing, dressing, and eating. Used to assess how independent someone is.
adrenaline: A hormone and emergency medication. Given by injection in anaphylaxis and cardiac arrest to increase heart rate and blood pressure.
AF: Atrial Fibrillation — an irregular, often fast heartbeat caused by chaotic electrical signals in the heart.
AKI: Acute Kidney Injury — when the kidneys suddenly stop working properly, often reversible if caught early.
albumin: A protein made by the liver. Low levels can indicate liver disease, kidney disease, or malnutrition.
alkalosis: When the blood becomes too alkaline (pH above 7.45). Can cause tingling, muscle cramps, and confusion.
ALT: Alanine Aminotransferase — a liver enzyme. High levels suggest liver damage.
amiodarone: An anti-arrhythmic drug used for serious heart rhythm problems. Has many side effects with long-term use.
amlodipine: A calcium channel blocker used for high blood pressure and angina.
amoxicillin: A common antibiotic used for chest infections, ear infections, and urinary infections. Often the first-choice antibiotic in the UK.
amylase: An enzyme made by the pancreas. Very high levels suggest pancreatitis (inflamed pancreas).
anaemia: A condition where the blood doesn't carry enough oxygen, usually due to low haemoglobin. Causes tiredness, pallor, and breathlessness.
analgesia: Pain relief — the umbrella term for all painkillers, from paracetamol to morphine.
anaphylaxis: A severe, life-threatening allergic reaction that can cause breathing difficulty, swelling, and dangerously low blood pressure.
angina: Chest pain caused by reduced blood flow to the heart muscle. Usually triggered by exertion and relieved by rest or GTN spray.
angiography: An imaging test that uses dye and X-rays (or CT) to look at blood vessels. Used to find blockages or aneurysms.
anion gap: A calculation from blood tests that helps work out the cause of acidosis. A high anion gap suggests toxins, ketones, or lactate.
anterior: Towards the front of the body.
anticoagulation: Blood-thinning treatment to prevent or treat blood clots (e.g. heparin, warfarin, DOACs).
antiemetic: A medication to prevent or treat nausea and vomiting (e.g. ondansetron, cyclizine).
aortic dissection: A tear in the wall of the aorta (the main artery). Causes tearing chest or back pain and can be rapidly fatal.
aortic regurgitation: A leaky aortic heart valve that allows blood to flow backwards. Causes a wide pulse pressure and a murmur.
aortic stenosis: Narrowing of the aortic heart valve, making the heart work harder to pump blood. Causes breathlessness, chest pain, and fainting.
apex beat: The point on the chest where the heartbeat is most easily felt. Displacement suggests an enlarged heart.
apical: Relating to the apex (tip) — often used to describe the top of the lung or the tip of the heart.
apixaban: A direct oral anticoagulant (DOAC) used to prevent stroke in AF and treat blood clots. Doesn't need regular blood monitoring.
APTT: Activated Partial Thromboplastin Time — a blood clotting test, often used to monitor heparin treatment.
ARB: Angiotensin Receptor Blocker — another type of blood pressure medication (e.g. losartan, candesartan).
arrhythmia: An abnormal heart rhythm — can be too fast, too slow, or irregular.
arterial line: A thin tube placed into an artery (usually the wrist) for continuous blood pressure monitoring and easy blood sampling in critically ill patients.
ascites: Abnormal build-up of fluid in the abdomen. Common in liver disease, cancer, and heart failure.
aspirin: A blood-thinning and anti-inflammatory drug. Given as 300mg loading dose in suspected heart attack or stroke.
aspirin loading: Giving a large initial dose of aspirin (usually 300mg) to quickly thin the blood, typically in suspected heart attack or stroke.
asthma: A long-term condition where the airways become inflamed and narrow, causing wheeze, breathlessness, and cough. Can be life-threatening in acute attacks.
ataxia: Loss of coordination and balance, making walking and movement unsteady.
atenolol: A beta-blocker used for high blood pressure and to control heart rate.
atorvastatin: A statin — a cholesterol-lowering medication that reduces the risk of heart attacks and strokes.
atrial flutter: An abnormal heart rhythm similar to AF but more organised. The atria beat very fast in a regular pattern, often at around 150 beats per minute.
atypical: Not following the usual pattern. An atypical presentation means the symptoms are unusual for that condition.
AVPU: A quick consciousness scale: Alert, responds to Voice, responds to Pain, Unresponsive.
axilla: The armpit. Sometimes used as a site for temperature measurement or lymph node examination.
base excess: A blood gas measurement showing how much acid or base is in the blood. Negative values mean excess acid.
base rate neglect: A thinking error where a doctor ignores how common or rare a condition is when making a diagnosis.
BD: Twice daily — a prescription abbreviation for how often to take a medication.
benzodiazepines: A class of sedative medications (e.g. diazepam, lorazepam) used for anxiety, seizures, and alcohol withdrawal.
beta-blocker: A type of medication that slows the heart rate and lowers blood pressure (e.g. bisoprolol, atenolol, propranolol).
bilateral: Affecting both sides of the body.
biliary colic: Pain caused by a gallstone temporarily blocking the bile duct. Causes intense right upper abdominal pain, often after fatty food.
bilirubin: A yellow substance made when red blood cells break down. High levels cause jaundice (yellow skin/eyes).
biphasic reaction: A second wave of allergic symptoms that can occur hours after the initial anaphylaxis episode, even after treatment.
bisoprolol: A beta-blocker used for heart failure, high blood pressure, and to control heart rate in AF.
Blatchford score: A scoring system to assess the severity of upper GI bleeding and whether the patient needs urgent intervention.
bleep: A pager carried by hospital doctors. Other staff 'bleep' a doctor to request a call back. Still widely used in UK hospitals.
blood cultures: Blood samples taken to detect bacteria in the bloodstream. Two sets are taken from different sites before starting antibiotics.
blood film: A sample of blood spread on a glass slide and examined under a microscope. Can reveal abnormal cells, parasites, or clotting problems.
blood gas: See ABG — a test measuring oxygen, carbon dioxide, and acid levels, usually from an artery.
blood transfusion: Giving donated blood (or blood products) through a drip. Used for severe bleeding or very low haemoglobin.
BMI: Body Mass Index — a measure of weight relative to height. Over 25 is overweight; over 30 is obese.
BNP: B-type Natriuretic Peptide — a blood test for heart failure. High levels mean the heart is under strain.
bowel sounds: Gurgling noises heard through a stethoscope on the abdomen. Absent sounds may suggest bowel obstruction or peritonitis.
BP: Blood Pressure — the force of blood pushing against artery walls. Written as systolic/diastolic (e.g. 120/80).
bradycardia: An abnormally slow heart rate, usually below 60 beats per minute.
bronchiectasis: Permanent widening and damage to the airways, causing chronic cough with lots of sputum. Often from repeated infections.
bronchospasm: Sudden tightening of the muscles around the airways, causing wheezing and difficulty breathing. Seen in asthma and allergic reactions.
Burch-Wartofsky score: A scoring system used to assess the likelihood of thyroid storm — a life-threatening overactivity of the thyroid gland.
CA125: A blood marker that can be raised in ovarian cancer, though it can also be elevated in other conditions.
calcium gluconate: Given IV as an emergency treatment for dangerously high potassium — it protects the heart while other treatments lower potassium.
cannula: A thin tube inserted into a vein to give fluids or medications directly into the bloodstream.
cannulation: The process of inserting a cannula (thin plastic tube) into a vein.
capillary refill: A test where you press on a fingernail and count how long it takes for the colour to return. Over 2 seconds suggests poor circulation.
carbimazole: A medication that blocks thyroid hormone production. Used for overactive thyroid (hyperthyroidism).
cardiac arrest: When the heart stops pumping blood. Requires immediate CPR and defibrillation. Not the same as a heart attack.
cardiac syncope: Fainting caused by a heart problem, such as an abnormal rhythm. More dangerous than a simple faint.
cardiogenic shock: When the heart is too weak to pump enough blood to the body. A medical emergency usually caused by a massive heart attack.
cardioversion: A procedure to restore a normal heart rhythm, either with medication or an electrical shock.
catheterisation: Inserting a flexible tube (catheter) into the bladder to drain urine. Done when a patient cannot pass urine or to monitor output.
cauda equina: A bundle of nerves at the base of the spinal cord. Compression is a surgical emergency that can cause permanent paralysis and bladder problems.
CCU: Coronary Care Unit — a specialised ward for patients with heart attacks and serious heart conditions.
cellulitis: A bacterial skin infection causing redness, warmth, swelling, and pain. Usually treated with antibiotics.
central line: A long IV line inserted into a large vein (usually the neck, chest, or groin) for giving strong medications, fluids, or monitoring.
ceftriaxone: A strong IV antibiotic commonly used for serious infections like meningitis and sepsis.
CHA2DS2-VASc: A scoring system that estimates stroke risk in patients with atrial fibrillation, used to decide if blood thinners are needed.
chest drain: A tube inserted through the chest wall to drain air (pneumothorax) or fluid (pleural effusion) from around the lung.
chlorphenamine: An antihistamine used in allergic reactions and anaphylaxis, alongside adrenaline.
cholangitis: Infection of the bile ducts, usually caused by a gallstone blockage. Can be life-threatening.
cholecystectomy: Surgical removal of the gallbladder. Usually done by keyhole surgery.
cholecystitis: Inflammation of the gallbladder, usually caused by gallstones. Causes severe right-sided abdominal pain.
chronic: Long-term, lasting weeks to years. The opposite of acute.
ciprofloxacin: A fluoroquinolone antibiotic used for urinary infections, some chest infections, and gastroenteritis.
CIWA-Ar: Clinical Institute Withdrawal Assessment — a scoring tool to measure the severity of alcohol withdrawal.
CKD: Chronic Kidney Disease — long-term, gradual loss of kidney function.
clarithromycin: A macrolide antibiotic often added alongside amoxicillin for moderate-to-severe pneumonia to cover atypical organisms.
clerking: The process of taking a patient's history, examining them, and forming an initial management plan on admission.
clopidogrel: An antiplatelet medication that prevents blood clots. Often given alongside aspirin after a heart attack or stent.
co-amoxiclav: A combination antibiotic (amoxicillin + clavulanic acid) used for a wide range of infections.
codeine: A mild opioid painkiller, often combined with paracetamol (co-codamol). Can cause constipation.
cognitive bias: A systematic thinking error that can lead to incorrect diagnoses. Everyone has them — the key is learning to recognise them.
COHb: Carboxyhaemoglobin — the level of carbon monoxide bound to red blood cells. Elevated in carbon monoxide poisoning.
collateral history: Information about a patient obtained from family, carers, or friends — especially useful when the patient is confused or unconscious.
consolidation: An area of the lung that has filled with fluid, pus, or blood instead of air. Seen on X-ray in pneumonia.
consultant: The most senior doctor in a hospital team, fully qualified as a specialist.
COPD: Chronic Obstructive Pulmonary Disease — a long-term lung condition causing breathlessness, usually from smoking.
co-oximetry: A specialised blood test that can detect carbon monoxide and other abnormal forms of haemoglobin that normal pulse oximeters miss.
cor pulmonale: Right-sided heart failure caused by long-term lung disease. The right side of the heart fails because it has to pump against high pressure in the lungs.
creatinine: A waste product filtered by the kidneys. Rising levels mean the kidneys aren't working well.
cremasteric reflex: A reflex where stroking the inner thigh causes the testicle to rise. Absence suggests testicular torsion.
crepitations: Crackling sounds heard through a stethoscope when breathing in, caused by fluid or secretions in the small airways. Also called crackles.
crossmatch: A blood test to check that donated blood is compatible with the patient's blood before a transfusion.
CRP: C-Reactive Protein — a blood marker of inflammation. High levels suggest infection or tissue damage.
CT: Computed Tomography — a detailed X-ray scan that creates cross-section images of the body.
CT contrast: A special dye injected during a CT scan to make blood vessels and organs show up more clearly. Can affect kidney function.
CTG: Cardiotocography — a monitor used in pregnancy to track the baby's heart rate and the mother's contractions.
CTPA: CT Pulmonary Angiogram — a specialised CT scan to look for blood clots in the lungs (pulmonary embolism).
CURB-65: A severity score for pneumonia: Confusion, Urea, Respiratory rate, Blood pressure, age ≥65. Higher score = more severe.
CVP: Central Venous Pressure — a measurement of pressure in the large veins near the heart, used to assess fluid status in critically ill patients.
CXR: Chest X-Ray — an image of the lungs and heart used to detect pneumonia, fluid, fractures, and other problems.
cyanosis: A bluish discolouration of the skin and lips caused by low oxygen levels in the blood.
cyclizine: An anti-sickness medication commonly used in UK hospitals.
D-dimer: A blood test that detects clot breakdown products. A negative result helps rule out blood clots (PE/DVT).
dapagliflozin: An SGLT2 inhibitor used for diabetes, heart failure, and kidney disease. Can cause urinary infections.
decompensated: When a condition that was previously controlled becomes unstable — the body can no longer compensate. Often used for heart failure or liver disease.
defibrillation: Delivering an electrical shock to the heart to restore a normal rhythm during cardiac arrest or dangerous arrhythmias.
delirium: Sudden confusion and altered awareness, usually caused by an underlying medical problem (infection, medication, dehydration). Reversible.
delirium tremens: A severe, potentially fatal form of alcohol withdrawal causing confusion, hallucinations, seizures, and cardiovascular instability.
dementia: A long-term, progressive decline in memory and thinking. Unlike delirium, it develops slowly over months to years.
dexamethasone: A strong steroid used in croup, raised intracranial pressure, and spinal cord compression.
DHx: Drug History — a record of all medications a patient is currently taking, including doses and frequency.
diaphoresis: Profuse sweating, often a sign of a serious problem like heart attack, shock, or hypoglycaemia.
diazepam: A benzodiazepine sedative used for anxiety, seizures, muscle spasms, and alcohol withdrawal.
DIC: Disseminated Intravascular Coagulation — a dangerous condition where blood clots form throughout the body, using up clotting factors and causing bleeding.
differential diagnosis: A list of possible conditions that could explain a patient's symptoms, ranked from most to least likely.
digoxin: A medication that slows heart rate in atrial fibrillation and strengthens heart contractions in heart failure.
diplopia: Double vision — seeing two images of a single object. Can indicate neurological problems, thyroid eye disease, or stroke.
discharge summary: A document written when a patient leaves hospital, summarising their diagnosis, treatment, and plan for the GP.
distal: Further from the centre of the body. The hand is distal to the elbow.
diverticulitis: Inflammation or infection of small pouches (diverticula) in the bowel wall. Causes left-sided lower abdominal pain, fever, and altered bowel habit.
DKA: Diabetic Ketoacidosis — a serious complication of diabetes where the body produces excess ketones (acids). A medical emergency.
DNAR: Do Not Attempt Resuscitation — a medical decision that CPR should not be performed if the heart stops. Made after discussion with patient or family.
DOAC: Direct Oral Anticoagulant — newer blood thinners (e.g. apixaban, rivarelbaan) that don't need regular blood monitoring.
dorsal: Relating to the back of the body or a body part.
doxycycline: An antibiotic used for chest infections (especially atypical pneumonia), skin infections, and malaria prevention.
DVT: Deep Vein Thrombosis — a blood clot in a deep vein, usually in the leg. Can break off and travel to the lungs (PE).
dysphagia: Difficulty swallowing. Can be caused by stroke, oesophageal cancer, or neurological conditions.
dyspnoea: Breathlessness or difficulty breathing. A symptom of many heart and lung conditions.
dysuria: Pain or burning when urinating. A common symptom of urinary tract infections.
ECG: Electrocardiogram — a test that records the heart's electrical activity using stickers on the chest. Shows rhythm problems and heart attacks.
echocardiography: An ultrasound scan of the heart that shows how well it's pumping and whether the valves are working properly.
eclampsia: Seizures occurring in pregnancy due to severe pre-eclampsia. A life-threatening emergency requiring immediate delivery.
ectopic pregnancy: When a pregnancy implants outside the womb, usually in a fallopian tube. Can rupture and cause life-threatening internal bleeding.
effusion: Abnormal fluid collection. A pleural effusion is fluid around the lungs; a pericardial effusion is fluid around the heart.
eGFR: Estimated Glomerular Filtration Rate — a measure of kidney function. Below 60 suggests kidney disease.
ejection fraction: The percentage of blood pumped out by the heart with each beat. Normal is 55-70%. Below 40% indicates heart failure.
electrolytes: Minerals in the blood (sodium, potassium, calcium, etc.) essential for nerve, muscle, and heart function.
embolism: A blood clot or other material that travels through the bloodstream and blocks a vessel elsewhere (e.g. pulmonary embolism).
empyema: A collection of pus in the space around the lung (pleural space). A serious complication of pneumonia requiring drainage.
endoscopy: A procedure using a camera on a flexible tube to look inside the body, commonly into the stomach (gastroscopy).
enoxaparin: A low molecular weight heparin given by injection under the skin to prevent or treat blood clots.
epididymitis: Inflammation of the tube behind the testicle (epididymis), usually from infection. Can mimic testicular torsion.
epilepsy: A neurological condition causing recurrent seizures (fits). Managed with daily anti-seizure medications.
erythema: Redness of the skin, caused by increased blood flow. Seen in infection, inflammation, and allergic reactions.
escalation: Asking for more senior help or moving a patient to a higher level of care when they are deteriorating.
ESR: Erythrocyte Sedimentation Rate — a blood test for inflammation. Rises more slowly than CRP and stays elevated longer.
exacerbation: A sudden worsening of a long-term condition, such as COPD or asthma.
extravasation: When IV fluids or medications leak out of the vein into surrounding tissue, causing swelling and potential tissue damage.
FAST: Face, Arms, Speech, Time — a quick test to recognise stroke symptoms. If any are abnormal, call 999.
FBC: Full Blood Count — a routine blood test measuring red cells, white cells, and platelets.
femoral: Relating to the femur (thigh bone) or the major blood vessels in the groin.
FHx: Family History — a record of medical conditions in a patient's close relatives.
fibrinogen: A protein involved in blood clotting. Low levels (as in DIC) increase the risk of dangerous bleeding.
FIT test: Faecal Immunochemical Test — checks for hidden blood in stool, used as a screening test for bowel cancer.
flucloxacillin: An antibiotic specifically for skin infections like cellulitis. Usually taken four times a day.
fluid resuscitation: Giving IV fluids rapidly to restore blood volume and blood pressure, usually in shock or severe dehydration.
flumazenil: The antidote for benzodiazepine overdose. Rarely used because it can cause seizures.
focal neurology: Neurological signs that point to a specific area of brain damage — e.g. weakness on one side, speech problems, or visual loss.
Fournier's gangrene: A severe, life-threatening infection of the genital and perineal area. A type of necrotising fasciitis requiring emergency surgery.
furosemide: A loop diuretic ('water tablet') that makes you pass more urine. Used in heart failure and fluid overload.
FY1: Foundation Year 1 doctor — a junior doctor in their first year after medical school.
FY2: Foundation Year 2 doctor — a junior doctor in their second year after medical school.
gallstones: Hard deposits that form in the gallbladder. Can cause biliary colic, cholecystitis, or cholangitis if they block the bile ducts.
GCS: Glasgow Coma Scale — a scoring system (3-15) that measures consciousness based on eye, verbal, and motor responses.
gentamicin: A powerful IV antibiotic used for serious infections like sepsis. Requires careful dosing because it can damage the kidneys and hearing.
gliclazide: A diabetes medication that stimulates the pancreas to produce more insulin. Can cause low blood sugar.
glucose: Blood sugar. Normal fasting level is 4-7 mmol/L. High levels may indicate diabetes or stress response.
glycaemic control: How well a person's blood sugar levels are managed. Good control reduces the risk of diabetes complications.
GORD: Gastro-Oesophageal Reflux Disease — when stomach acid repeatedly flows back into the oesophagus, causing heartburn and sometimes a chronic cough.
group and save: A blood test that determines the patient's blood group and screens for antibodies. Done in case a transfusion is needed later.
GTN: Glyceryl Trinitrate — a spray or tablet placed under the tongue for angina (chest pain from the heart). Opens up blood vessels.
guarding: When a patient tenses their abdominal muscles during examination, often involuntarily. Suggests inflammation of the abdominal lining (peritonitis).
Hb: Haemoglobin — the protein in red blood cells that carries oxygen. Low levels mean anaemia.
HbA1c: Glycated Haemoglobin — a blood test showing average blood sugar control over the last 2-3 months. Used to diagnose and monitor diabetes.
hCG: Human Chorionic Gonadotropin — a hormone produced in pregnancy. Used in pregnancy tests and to monitor ectopic pregnancy.
HCO3: Bicarbonate — a blood buffer that helps maintain normal pH. Low levels indicate acidosis.
heart block: A condition where electrical signals in the heart are delayed or blocked, causing the heart to beat too slowly. May need a pacemaker.
HELLP: A severe complication of pregnancy involving Haemolysis (red blood cell destruction), Elevated Liver enzymes, and Low Platelets.
heparin: A blood thinner given by injection or IV drip. Used for blood clots and during some procedures.
hepatomegaly: An enlarged liver, which can be felt below the rib cage on the right side. Caused by liver disease, heart failure, or cancer.
hernia: When an organ or tissue pushes through a weakness in the surrounding muscle or tissue wall. Can become dangerous if strangulated.
HHS: Hyperosmolar Hyperglycaemic State — a serious diabetes complication with very high blood sugar and severe dehydration, but without significant ketones.
HR: Heart Rate — the number of times the heart beats per minute. Normal adult range is 60-100.
Hx: Abbreviation for history — as in 'patient history'.
hydrocortisone: A steroid medication used to treat adrenal crisis, severe allergic reactions, and inflammatory conditions.
hypercapnia: Too much carbon dioxide in the blood, often from breathing failure. Causes drowsiness and confusion.
hyperglycaemia: High blood sugar (above 7 mmol/L fasting or 11 mmol/L random).
hyperkalaemia: Dangerously high potassium levels in the blood. Can cause fatal heart rhythm problems.
hypertension: High blood pressure — sustained readings above 140/90 mmHg.
hyperthyroidism: An overactive thyroid gland that produces too much thyroid hormone. Causes weight loss, rapid heartbeat, anxiety, tremor, and heat intolerance. Treated with carbimazole or propylthiouracil.
hypocalcaemia: Low calcium levels in the blood. Can cause tingling, muscle cramps, and in severe cases, seizures or heart rhythm problems.
hypoglycaemia: Low blood sugar (below 4 mmol/L). Causes sweating, shaking, confusion, and can be dangerous.
hypokalaemia: Low potassium levels in the blood. Can cause muscle weakness, cramps, and dangerous heart rhythm problems.
hyponatraemia: Low sodium levels in the blood. Can cause confusion, seizures, and brain swelling if severe.
hypotension: Low blood pressure — can cause dizziness, fainting, and in severe cases, organ failure.
hypothermia: Dangerously low body temperature (below 35°C). Causes confusion, shivering, and can lead to cardiac arrest.
hypothyroidism: An underactive thyroid gland that doesn't produce enough thyroid hormone. Causes tiredness, weight gain, feeling cold, and dry skin. Treated with levothyroxine.
hypovolaemia: Low blood volume, usually from bleeding or severe dehydration. Causes low blood pressure and fast heart rate.
hypoxia: Dangerously low oxygen levels in the body's tissues. Can cause confusion, agitation, and organ damage.
ibuprofen: An anti-inflammatory painkiller (NSAID). Should be avoided in kidney disease, asthma, and stomach ulcers.
ileus: When the bowel stops moving temporarily, usually after surgery or due to medication. Different from a mechanical obstruction.
IM: Intramuscular — an injection given into a muscle (e.g. adrenaline in anaphylaxis).
inattentional blindness: A thinking error where a doctor misses an important finding because their attention is focused elsewhere.
incidental finding: Something discovered during a test that wasn't what the test was looking for — e.g. finding a lung nodule on a chest X-ray done for something else.
INR: International Normalised Ratio — a blood test measuring how long blood takes to clot. Used to monitor warfarin.
insulin: A hormone that lowers blood sugar. Given by injection in Type 1 diabetes and sometimes Type 2 diabetes.
insulin-dextrose: An emergency IV treatment for high potassium — insulin drives potassium into cells, dextrose prevents low blood sugar.
intubation: Placing a breathing tube through the mouth into the windpipe (trachea) to keep the airway open and allow a ventilator to breathe for the patient.
ipratropium: A bronchodilator given by nebuliser alongside salbutamol in acute asthma and COPD exacerbations.
ischaemia: When an organ or tissue does not get enough blood supply and oxygen. Can cause damage if prolonged.
ITU: Intensive Treatment Unit (also called ICU) — the ward for the most critically ill patients who need organ support like ventilators.
IV: Intravenous — given directly into a vein through a cannula or drip.
Ix: Abbreviation for investigations — the tests ordered to help make a diagnosis.
jaundice: Yellowing of the skin and whites of the eyes caused by a build-up of bilirubin. Can indicate liver disease, bile duct blockage, or haemolysis.
JVP: Jugular Venous Pressure — the height of the vein pulsation visible in the neck. Raised in heart failure and fluid overload.
K: Potassium — an electrolyte essential for heart and muscle function. Both high and low levels are dangerous.
ketonaemia: Excess ketones (acids) in the blood, seen in diabetic ketoacidosis and starvation.
Korsakoff: Korsakoff's syndrome — permanent brain damage from untreated Wernicke's encephalopathy, causing severe memory problems.
lactate: A substance produced when cells don't get enough oxygen. High levels suggest the body's tissues are under stress, often from sepsis or shock.
lansoprazole: A proton pump inhibitor similar to omeprazole.
laparoscopy: Keyhole surgery — operating through small incisions using a camera and specialised instruments.
laparotomy: Open surgery on the abdomen through a large incision. Done as an emergency when there is serious abdominal pathology.
lateral: Towards the side of the body, away from the midline.
leukocytes: White blood cells. When found in urine, they suggest infection or inflammation in the urinary tract.
levothyroxine: Thyroid hormone replacement for underactive thyroid (hypothyroidism). Taken daily on an empty stomach.
LFTs: Liver Function Tests — blood tests that check how well the liver is working.
lipase: A pancreatic enzyme. Very high levels strongly suggest pancreatitis.
loading dose: A larger initial dose of a medication given to quickly reach effective levels in the body, followed by smaller maintenance doses.
locum: A temporary doctor filling in for another. Common in UK hospitals and GP surgeries.
lorazepam: A benzodiazepine commonly used as the first-line treatment for prolonged seizures.
LP: Lumbar Puncture — a procedure to collect spinal fluid by inserting a needle into the lower back. Used to diagnose meningitis and SAH.
LRINEC score: Laboratory Risk Indicator for Necrotising Fasciitis — a scoring system to help distinguish severe soft tissue infections from simple cellulitis.
Lugol's iodine: An iodine solution given in thyroid storm to block the release of thyroid hormones. Given after anti-thyroid drugs.
lymphadenopathy: Swollen lymph nodes (glands). Can indicate infection, inflammation, or cancer.
magnesium sulfate: A medication used to prevent seizures in pre-eclampsia and to treat severe asthma and certain heart rhythm problems.
mast cell tryptase: A blood test taken after a suspected allergic reaction to confirm whether anaphylaxis occurred.
McBurney's point: A point in the right lower abdomen that is classically tender in appendicitis.
MDT: Multidisciplinary Team — a group of different healthcare professionals (doctors, nurses, physios, etc.) who work together on a patient's care.
medial: Towards the middle of the body.
melaena: Black, tarry, foul-smelling stools caused by bleeding in the upper gastrointestinal tract. A sign of a serious GI bleed.
meninges: The protective membranes that surround the brain and spinal cord. Inflammation of these is called meningitis.
meningism: Signs of meningeal irritation: neck stiffness, headache, and sensitivity to light. Suggests meningitis or SAH.
meropenem: A very broad-spectrum IV antibiotic reserved for serious infections that don't respond to other antibiotics.
metabolic acidosis: Acidosis caused by a build-up of acid (e.g. lactate, ketones) or loss of bicarbonate. Common in sepsis, DKA, and kidney failure.
metformin: The first-line medication for Type 2 diabetes. Works by reducing sugar production in the liver and improving insulin sensitivity.
metronidazole: An antibiotic used for anaerobic infections (bacteria that thrive without oxygen), abdominal infections, and C. diff.
midazolam: A benzodiazepine given buccally (inside the cheek) for seizures in the community, or IV for sedation.
midstream urine: A urine sample collected mid-flow (not the first or last part) to reduce contamination. Sent for culture to identify infections.
mitral regurgitation: A leaky mitral heart valve that allows blood to flow backwards into the left atrium. Causes a murmur and can lead to heart failure.
mitral stenosis: Narrowing of the mitral heart valve, restricting blood flow through the heart. Often caused by previous rheumatic fever.
morphine: A strong opioid painkiller used for severe pain, heart attacks, and palliative care. Can cause drowsiness and slow breathing.
MRI: Magnetic Resonance Imaging — a detailed scan using magnets and radio waves. Better than CT for soft tissues, brain, and spine.
MRCP: Magnetic Resonance Cholangiopancreatography — an MRI scan specifically looking at the bile ducts and pancreatic duct.
MRSA: Methicillin-Resistant Staphylococcus Aureus — a type of bacteria resistant to many common antibiotics. Requires specific treatment.
Murphy's sign: Pain on pressing under the right rib cage that stops the patient breathing in. A clinical sign of cholecystitis.
murmur: An extra or unusual heart sound heard through a stethoscope, caused by turbulent blood flow. May indicate a valve problem.
Mx: Abbreviation for management — the treatment plan for a patient.
Na: Sodium — an electrolyte important for fluid balance and nerve/muscle function.
NAC: N-Acetylcysteine — the antidote for paracetamol overdose, given as an IV drip.
naloxone: The antidote for opioid overdose. Reverses the effects of morphine, heroin, and other opioids within minutes.
nebulised: Medication turned into a fine mist and breathed in through a mask, often used for asthma (e.g. salbutamol).
necrotising fasciitis: A rare but life-threatening infection that destroys tissue under the skin. Spreads rapidly and needs emergency surgery.
needle decompression: Emergency insertion of a large needle into the chest to release trapped air in a tension pneumothorax.
nephrotoxic: Harmful to the kidneys. Some medications (e.g. NSAIDs, gentamicin) can damage kidney function.
neutrophils: The most common type of white blood cell, important for fighting bacterial infections. High levels suggest infection.
NEWS2: National Early Warning Score 2 — a scoring system using vital signs to detect deteriorating patients. Score ≥5 needs urgent review; ≥7 is critical.
NGT: Nasogastric Tube — a tube passed through the nose into the stomach, used to drain stomach contents or give nutrition.
NIV: Non-Invasive Ventilation — a breathing support machine using a tight-fitting mask, used in COPD exacerbations and respiratory failure.
nitrites: A substance detected on urine dipstick that suggests bacterial infection (bacteria convert nitrate to nitrite).
nitrofurantoin: An antibiotic specifically for uncomplicated urinary tract infections. Not suitable if kidney function is poor.
non-blanching rash: A rash that doesn't fade when pressed (glass test). Can indicate meningococcal sepsis — a medical emergency.
normalcy bias: A thinking error where a doctor underestimates the severity of a situation, assuming things are probably fine.
NSAID: Non-Steroidal Anti-Inflammatory Drug (e.g. ibuprofen, naproxen). Reduces pain and inflammation but can harm kidneys and stomach.
NSTEMI: Non-ST Elevation Myocardial Infarction — a type of heart attack that doesn't show the classic ST elevation on ECG but still damages heart muscle.
NT-proBNP: N-terminal pro B-type Natriuretic Peptide — a blood test for heart failure. More sensitive than BNP.
nystagmus: Involuntary, rapid eye movements. Can indicate inner ear problems, stroke, or neurological disease.
obs: Observations — routine measurements of vital signs including heart rate, blood pressure, temperature, respiratory rate, and oxygen levels.
occult: Hidden — not immediately obvious. An occult fracture doesn't show on a normal X-ray; occult blood is blood not visible to the naked eye.
oedema: Swelling caused by fluid build-up in the body's tissues. Common in the ankles (peripheral oedema) and lungs (pulmonary oedema).
O/E: On Examination — what the doctor found when physically examining the patient.
omeprazole: A proton pump inhibitor (PPI) that reduces stomach acid. Used for ulcers, reflux, and alongside NSAIDs.
on-call: The period when a doctor is available to deal with emergencies and urgent problems outside normal working hours.
ondansetron: An anti-sickness medication commonly used in hospitals. Given orally, IV, or as a wafer that dissolves on the tongue.
ophthalmoplegia: Paralysis or weakness of the eye muscles, causing double vision or inability to move the eyes normally.
opioid: A class of strong painkillers including morphine, codeine, and fentanyl. Can cause drowsiness, constipation, and slowed breathing.
orthopaedics: The medical speciality dealing with bones, joints, and muscles.
orthopnoea: Breathlessness when lying flat, relieved by sitting up. A classic symptom of heart failure.
osmolality: A measure of the concentration of dissolved particles in blood. Very high in HHS (dehydration).
Pabrinex: An IV vitamin preparation containing thiamine (B1) and other B vitamins. Given to treat or prevent Wernicke's encephalopathy.
pacemaker: A small device implanted under the skin that sends electrical impulses to the heart to maintain a regular heartbeat.
pallor: Unusual paleness of the skin. Can indicate anaemia, shock, or poor circulation.
palpitation: An awareness of your own heartbeat — it may feel fast, irregular, or pounding. Can be harmless or indicate an arrhythmia.
pancreatitis: Inflammation of the pancreas, causing severe upper abdominal pain. Often caused by gallstones or alcohol.
papilloedema: Swelling of the optic disc at the back of the eye, seen in dangerously high blood pressure or raised brain pressure.
paracentesis: A procedure to drain fluid (ascites) from the abdomen using a needle or drain. Done for symptom relief or diagnostic testing.
paracetamol: A common painkiller and fever reducer. Safe at normal doses but dangerous in overdose — can cause fatal liver damage.
paraesthesia: Abnormal sensations like tingling, numbness, or 'pins and needles'. Can indicate nerve compression or damage.
pCO2: Partial pressure of carbon dioxide in the blood. High levels mean the lungs aren't removing CO2 effectively.
PE: Pulmonary Embolism — a blood clot in the lungs. Causes sudden breathlessness, chest pain, and can be fatal.
PEF: Peak Expiratory Flow — a measure of how fast you can blow air out. Low readings indicate asthma severity.
percutaneous: Through the skin — describes a procedure done by inserting a needle or wire through the skin rather than open surgery.
pericardial effusion: Fluid around the heart, which can compress it and prevent it filling properly. Large effusions are an emergency.
pericarditis: Inflammation of the sac around the heart (pericardium). Causes sharp chest pain that worsens with breathing and lying flat.
pericardium: The thin sac of tissue surrounding the heart.
perineum: The area between the genitals and the anus.
perioperative: Around the time of surgery — before, during, and after the operation.
peripheral: Relating to the limbs or outer parts of the body, as opposed to the central (core) parts.
peritoneum: The thin membrane lining the inside of the abdomen and covering the abdominal organs.
peritonitis: Inflammation of the peritoneum, usually from infection or bowel perforation. Causes severe abdominal pain and rigidity. A surgical emergency.
permissive hypotension: Deliberately keeping blood pressure slightly low in certain emergencies (e.g. ruptured AAA) to reduce bleeding until surgery.
petechiae: Tiny pinpoint red or purple spots on the skin caused by bleeding under the skin. Can indicate low platelets or meningococcal infection.
pH: A measure of acidity. Normal blood pH is 7.35-7.45. Below 7.35 is acidosis; above 7.45 is alkalosis.
phenytoin: An anti-seizure medication given IV when benzodiazepines fail to stop status epilepticus.
phlebotomy: Taking blood samples from a vein for testing. Also refers to therapeutic blood removal in certain conditions.
piperacillin-tazobactam: A broad-spectrum IV antibiotic (often called 'pip-taz' or Tazocin) used for serious hospital infections and sepsis.
platelets: Blood cells that help form clots. Low levels (thrombocytopenia) increase bleeding risk.
pleura: The two-layered membrane surrounding each lung. The space between the layers can fill with fluid (effusion) or air (pneumothorax).
pleural effusion: Fluid collection between the lung and chest wall, which can compress the lung and cause breathlessness.
pleuritic: Pain that worsens with breathing — sharp, stabbing chest pain typically caused by inflammation of the pleura.
PMH: Past Medical History — a patient's previous medical conditions and diagnoses.
pneumonia: Infection of the lungs causing inflammation and fluid in the air sacs. Symptoms include cough, fever, and breathlessness.
pneumothorax: A collapsed lung caused by air leaking into the space around the lung. Tension pneumothorax is immediately life-threatening.
pO2: Partial pressure of oxygen in the blood. Low levels mean the blood isn't carrying enough oxygen.
polypharmacy: When a patient is taking many medications at once (usually five or more). Increases the risk of drug interactions and side effects.
posterior: Towards the back of the body.
post-ictal: The confused, drowsy state after a seizure. Can last minutes to hours.
PPI: Proton Pump Inhibitor — a medication that reduces stomach acid production (e.g. omeprazole, lansoprazole).
pre-eclampsia: A pregnancy complication causing high blood pressure and organ damage, usually after 20 weeks. Can progress to eclampsia (seizures).
prednisolone: A steroid tablet used for asthma attacks, COPD exacerbations, and inflammatory conditions. Usually a short course of 5 days.
premature closure: A thinking error where a doctor stops considering other diagnoses too early, accepting the first plausible answer.
PRN: As needed — a prescription abbreviation meaning take the medication only when required.
procalcitonin: A blood marker that rises specifically in bacterial infections. Helps distinguish bacterial from viral infection.
prognosis: The likely outcome or course of a disease — how the condition is expected to develop.
prophylaxis: Preventive treatment — given to stop a condition from occurring (e.g. antibiotics before surgery, VTE prevention).
propranolol: A beta-blocker used for anxiety, tremor, migraine prevention, and thyroid storm.
proteinuria: Protein in the urine, which can indicate kidney damage or pre-eclampsia in pregnancy.
proximal: Closer to the centre of the body. The shoulder is proximal to the elbow.
PTU: Propylthiouracil — a medication that blocks thyroid hormone production, used in thyroid storm.
pulmonary oedema: Fluid in the lungs, usually from heart failure. Causes severe breathlessness, particularly when lying flat.
pulse oximetry: A clip placed on the finger that measures blood oxygen levels (SpO2). Normal is 94-98%.
purpura: Purple spots or patches on the skin caused by bleeding underneath. Larger than petechiae. Can indicate clotting problems or vasculitis.
pyelonephritis: A kidney infection, more serious than a simple UTI. Causes loin pain, fever, and feeling very unwell.
QDS: Four times daily — a prescription abbreviation.
ramipril: An ACE inhibitor used for high blood pressure, heart failure, and kidney protection in diabetes. Can cause a dry cough.
rebound tenderness: Pain felt when releasing pressure on the abdomen rather than when pressing down. A sign of peritoneal inflammation (peritonitis).
rectal examination: A physical examination of the lower rectum by inserting a gloved finger. Used to check for bleeding, masses, and prostate problems.
registrar: A senior doctor in specialist training, more experienced than an FY1/FY2 but not yet a consultant.
renal colic: Severe pain caused by a kidney stone moving through the urinary tract. Typically comes in waves and radiates from loin to groin.
representativeness bias: A thinking error where a doctor expects a textbook presentation and misses atypical cases that don't fit the classic pattern.
respiratory acidosis: Acidosis caused by the lungs not removing enough carbon dioxide. Seen in COPD, severe asthma, and respiratory failure.
respiratory failure: When the lungs cannot maintain adequate oxygen levels or remove carbon dioxide effectively. Type 1 = low oxygen; Type 2 = low oxygen plus high CO2.
reticulocytes: Young red blood cells. High counts suggest the bone marrow is working hard to replace lost red cells (e.g. after bleeding or haemolysis).
rigidity: Involuntary stiffness of the abdominal muscles during examination. A more serious sign than guarding, suggesting peritonitis.
rigors: Intense shivering episodes with shaking and teeth chattering, caused by a sudden rise in body temperature. Often seen in serious infections.
rivaroxaban: A direct oral anticoagulant (DOAC) similar to apixaban. Used for AF, DVT, and PE.
Rockall score: A scoring system used after endoscopy to assess the risk of rebleeding and death from upper GI bleeding.
ROS: Review of Systems — a structured set of questions covering every body system to make sure nothing has been missed.
ROSIER: Recognition of Stroke in the Emergency Room — a clinical scoring tool used by doctors to assess whether symptoms are caused by a stroke.
RR: Respiratory Rate — the number of breaths per minute. Normal adult range is 12-20.
saddle anaesthesia: Loss of sensation in the area that would touch a saddle (inner thighs, buttocks, genitals). A red flag for cauda equina syndrome.
SAH: Subarachnoid Haemorrhage — bleeding on the surface of the brain, usually from a burst aneurysm. Causes sudden severe headache.
salbutamol: A bronchodilator inhaler (blue inhaler) that opens the airways. Used for asthma and COPD.
saturations: See SpO2 — the percentage of haemoglobin carrying oxygen, measured by a finger probe.
SC: Subcutaneous — an injection given just under the skin (e.g. insulin, enoxaparin).
seizure: A sudden burst of abnormal electrical activity in the brain causing involuntary movements, altered consciousness, or unusual sensations.
sepsis: A life-threatening condition where the body's response to an infection damages its own organs. Needs immediate treatment.
Sepsis Six: A bundle of six treatments to be given within one hour of suspecting sepsis: oxygen, blood cultures, IV antibiotics, IV fluids, lactate measurement, and urine output monitoring.
SGLT2i: A type of diabetes medication (e.g. dapagliflozin) that also benefits heart failure and kidney disease.
SHO: Senior House Officer — a junior doctor grade (now largely replaced by FY2/CT/ST grades in the UK, but the term is still used informally).
SHx: Social History — a record of a patient's lifestyle including smoking, alcohol, occupation, and living situation.
simvastatin: A statin used to lower cholesterol. Should be taken at night.
sinus tachycardia: A fast but regular heart rhythm originating from the heart's normal pacemaker. Often a response to pain, fever, or dehydration.
SIRS: Systemic Inflammatory Response Syndrome — signs that the body is mounting a major inflammatory response (fever, fast heart rate, fast breathing, abnormal white cells).
spironolactone: A potassium-sparing diuretic used in heart failure and liver disease. Can raise potassium levels.
splenomegaly: An enlarged spleen. Can be caused by infection, liver disease, blood disorders, or cancer.
SpO2: Blood oxygen saturation measured by pulse oximetry. Normal is 94-98%. Below 92% usually needs oxygen.
sputum: Phlegm coughed up from the lungs. Sent for culture to identify the organism causing a chest infection.
ST changes: Abnormalities on the ECG trace that can indicate heart attack, ischaemia, or other heart conditions.
stat: Immediately — a single dose given right now, as a one-off.
status epilepticus: A seizure lasting more than 5 minutes, or repeated seizures without recovery between them. A medical emergency.
STEMI: ST Elevation Myocardial Infarction — the most serious type of heart attack, showing characteristic changes on ECG. Needs emergency treatment.
stent: A small mesh tube inserted into a narrowed or blocked blood vessel (or bile duct) to hold it open.
sternotomy: An incision through the breastbone (sternum) to access the heart for surgery.
Stokes-Adams attack: A sudden fainting episode caused by the heart briefly stopping due to a rhythm disturbance, usually heart block.
strangulation: When the blood supply to a piece of bowel is cut off, usually by a hernia or adhesion. A surgical emergency.
stridor: A high-pitched breathing sound heard on breathing in, caused by narrowing of the upper airway. Can indicate a life-threatening obstruction.
stroke: Brain damage caused by a blocked or burst blood vessel in the brain. Causes sudden weakness, speech problems, or visual loss. A medical emergency.
subclavian: Relating to the area below the collarbone. The subclavian vein is used for central line insertion.
suprapubic: The area just above the pubic bone, in the lower abdomen. Tenderness here often suggests bladder or urinary problems.
SVT: Supraventricular Tachycardia — a fast heart rhythm (often over 150 bpm) originating above the ventricles. Causes palpitations and can be stopped with specific manoeuvres.
syncope: Fainting — a temporary loss of consciousness caused by reduced blood flow to the brain.
systolic: The top number of a blood pressure reading, representing the pressure when the heart contracts and pumps blood.
T2DM: Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus — a condition where the body doesn't use insulin properly, causing high blood sugar.
tachycardia: An abnormally fast heart rate, usually above 100 beats per minute.
tachypnoea: Abnormally fast breathing, usually above 20 breaths per minute.
tamponade: Cardiac tamponade — when fluid around the heart compresses it, preventing it from filling properly. A life-threatening emergency.
Tazocin: Brand name for piperacillin-tazobactam — a broad-spectrum IV antibiotic commonly used in UK hospitals for sepsis and serious infections.
TDS: Three times daily — a prescription abbreviation.
temp: Temperature — normal body temperature is around 36.5-37.5°C. Above 38°C is a fever.
tension pneumothorax: A life-threatening emergency where air is trapped under pressure in the chest, compressing the lung and pushing the heart to the opposite side.
thiamine: Vitamin B1 — essential for brain function. Deficiency (often from alcohol misuse) causes Wernicke's encephalopathy.
thoracentesis: A procedure to drain fluid from around the lung (pleural effusion) by inserting a needle through the chest wall. Also called a pleural tap.
thorax: The chest — the part of the body between the neck and the abdomen, containing the heart and lungs.
thrombectomy: A procedure to physically remove a blood clot, used in major strokes and some heart attacks.
thrombocytopenia: Low platelet count in the blood, increasing the risk of bleeding.
thrombolysis: Clot-busting treatment given as an injection to dissolve blood clots, used in stroke and some heart attacks.
thrombophilia: An inherited or acquired tendency for the blood to clot too easily, increasing the risk of DVT and PE.
thrombosis: The formation of a blood clot inside a blood vessel, partially or completely blocking blood flow.
thunderclap headache: A sudden, severe headache reaching maximum intensity within seconds. A red flag for subarachnoid haemorrhage.
thyrotoxicosis: The clinical state caused by excess thyroid hormones, whether from hyperthyroidism or other causes. Causes tremor, weight loss, and fast heart rate.
TIA: Transient Ischaemic Attack — a mini-stroke where symptoms resolve within 24 hours. A warning sign of future stroke.
torsion: Twisting — testicular torsion is when the testicle twists on its blood supply, cutting off circulation. A surgical emergency.
TPMT: Thiopurine Methyltransferase — an enzyme tested before starting thiopurine medications (e.g. azathioprine) to check for dangerous drug reactions.
tracheostomy: A surgical opening in the front of the neck into the windpipe (trachea), allowing breathing when the upper airway is blocked.
tranexamic acid: A medication that helps blood clot. Used for heavy bleeding, trauma, and post-partum haemorrhage.
tricuspid regurgitation: A leaky tricuspid heart valve. Often caused by right heart failure or pulmonary hypertension.
trimethoprim: An antibiotic for urinary tract infections. A common first-line choice in the UK.
troponin: A protein released when heart muscle is damaged. Elevated levels confirm a heart attack.
troponin rise: An increase in troponin levels on blood tests, indicating heart muscle damage. Serial tests taken hours apart show the trend.
TSH: Thyroid Stimulating Hormone — a blood test to check thyroid function. High TSH suggests underactive thyroid; low TSH suggests overactive.
two-week wait: An urgent NHS referral pathway where a patient with suspected cancer symptoms must be seen by a specialist within two weeks.
U&Es: Urea and Electrolytes — a routine blood test checking kidney function and salt/potassium levels.
ultrasound: A scan using sound waves to create images of internal organs. Commonly used for abdominal, pregnancy, and heart imaging. No radiation.
unilateral: Affecting only one side of the body.
urea: A waste product from protein breakdown, filtered by the kidneys. High levels suggest kidney problems or dehydration.
urinalysis: Testing urine with a dipstick to check for blood, protein, glucose, infection, and other abnormalities.
urosepsis: Sepsis caused by a urinary tract infection that has spread to the bloodstream.
UTI: Urinary Tract Infection — an infection in the bladder or kidneys causing pain, frequency, and sometimes confusion in elderly patients.
Valsalva manoeuvre: Bearing down as if straining on the toilet. Used as a technique to try to stop certain fast heart rhythms (SVT).
vancomycin: A strong IV antibiotic used for serious infections including MRSA. Requires blood level monitoring.
variceal bleeding: Bleeding from swollen veins (varices) in the oesophagus or stomach, usually caused by liver disease. Can be massive and life-threatening.
vasovagal: A common fainting episode caused by a sudden drop in heart rate and blood pressure, often triggered by standing, pain, or stress.
ventricular fibrillation: A life-threatening heart rhythm where the ventricles quiver instead of pumping. Requires immediate defibrillation.
ventricular tachycardia: A fast heart rhythm originating from the ventricles. Can be life-threatening and may deteriorate into cardiac arrest.
vertigo: A spinning sensation — feeling as though you or the room is moving. Caused by inner ear or brain problems.
VTE: Venous Thromboembolism — a blood clot in a vein, including DVT (leg) and PE (lungs).
ward round: A daily visit where the medical team reviews each patient on the ward, checking progress and updating plans.
warfarin: A blood thinner (anticoagulant) that requires regular INR blood monitoring to check the dose is right.
WBC: White Blood Cell count — immune cells that fight infection. High levels usually indicate infection or inflammation.
Wells score: A clinical scoring system used to estimate the probability of a blood clot (DVT or PE) before doing tests.
Wernicke's encephalopathy: A brain condition caused by thiamine (vitamin B1) deficiency, usually from alcohol misuse. Causes confusion, unsteady walking, and eye movement problems.
wheeze: A high-pitched whistling sound when breathing out, caused by narrowed airways. Common in asthma and COPD.
xanthochromia: A yellow discolouration of spinal fluid, indicating old bleeding. Used to diagnose subarachnoid haemorrhage when CT is negative.
anchoring bias: A thinking error where a doctor fixates on an early piece of information and fails to adjust their diagnosis as new evidence emerges.
availability bias: A thinking error where a doctor overestimates the likelihood of conditions they've seen recently or that are memorable.
premature closure: A thinking error where a doctor stops considering other diagnoses too early, accepting the first plausible answer.
base rate neglect: A thinking error where a doctor ignores how common or rare a condition is when making a diagnosis.
representativeness bias: A thinking error where a doctor expects a textbook presentation and misses atypical cases that don't fit the classic pattern.
normalcy bias: A thinking error where a doctor underestimates the severity of a situation, assuming things are probably fine.
inattentional blindness: A thinking error where a doctor misses an important finding because their attention is focused elsewhere.
cognitive bias: A systematic thinking error that can lead to incorrect diagnoses. Everyone has them — the key is learning to recognise them.
FAST: Face, Arms, Speech, Time — a quick test to recognise stroke symptoms. If any are abnormal, call 999.
ROSIER: Recognition of Stroke in the Emergency Room — a clinical scoring tool used by doctors to assess whether symptoms are caused by a stroke.
adrenaline: A hormone and emergency medication. Given by injection in anaphylaxis and cardiac arrest to increase heart rate and blood pressure.
biphasic reaction: A second wave of allergic symptoms that can occur hours after the initial anaphylaxis episode, even after treatment.
saddle anaesthesia: Loss of sensation in the area that would touch a saddle (inner thighs, buttocks, genitals). A red flag for cauda equina syndrome.
permissive hypotension: Deliberately keeping blood pressure slightly low in certain emergencies (e.g. ruptured AAA) to reduce bleeding until surgery.
4AT: A quick bedside test (four questions) to screen for delirium. Score ≥4 suggests delirium is likely.
hypoactive delirium: A type of delirium where the patient is quiet, withdrawn, and sleepy rather than agitated. Often missed because the patient isn't 'causing trouble'.
appendicitis: Inflammation of the appendix, causing pain that typically starts centrally then moves to the right lower abdomen. Usually needs surgical removal.
haematemesis: Vomiting blood. Can be bright red (fresh bleeding) or look like coffee grounds (older blood). A sign of upper GI bleeding.
haematuria: Blood in the urine. Can be visible (frank haematuria) or only detectable on testing (microscopic). Needs investigation to rule out cancer.
haemoptysis: Coughing up blood. Can indicate serious conditions including PE, lung cancer, or tuberculosis.
handover: The formal transfer of patient care from one doctor or team to another, including key clinical information and outstanding tasks.
abdomen: The part of the body between the chest and the pelvis, containing the stomach, intestines, liver, kidneys, and other organs.
confirmation bias: A thinking error where a doctor seeks out information that confirms their existing diagnosis and ignores evidence that contradicts it.
triage: The process of sorting patients by the urgency of their condition to decide who gets treated first.
resuscitation: Emergency treatment to restore breathing and circulation in someone who is critically ill or in cardiac arrest.
palliative: Treatment aimed at relieving symptoms and improving quality of life, rather than curing the disease.
benign: Not cancerous or life-threatening. The opposite of malignant.
malignant: Cancerous — a growth that can invade surrounding tissue and spread to other parts of the body.
metastasis: The spread of cancer from where it started to another part of the body, through the blood or lymph system.
biopsy: Taking a small sample of tissue for examination under a microscope, usually to check for cancer.
contraindication: A reason why a particular treatment or medication should NOT be given — because it could cause harm.
pruritus: Itching. Can be caused by skin conditions, liver disease, kidney failure, or medication side effects.
tinnitus: Ringing, buzzing, or hissing sounds in the ears with no external source. Can be caused by medication, noise damage, or Meniere's disease.
casualty: The emergency department of a hospital — where patients with urgent and life-threatening conditions are seen.
theatre: The operating theatre — the room where surgical operations are performed.
